title: Mass-Casualty Incident Triage at a Highway Bus Crash (~25 Casualties, Adults and Children) Using START / JumpSTART: Shifting from Individual-Patient Care to the Greatest Good for the Greatest Number, Rapidly Categorizing Casualties (Immediate/Red, Delayed/Yellow, Minimal/Green, Expectant-Deceased/Black), Performing Only Minimal Life-Saving Interventions During Triage, Working Within Incident Command, and Leading/Delegating the Response
subtitle: A MASS-CASUALTY INCIDENT (MCI) is an event in which the number/severity of casualties OVERWHELMS available resources, forcing a fundamental MINDSET SHIFT from everyday practice ('do everything possible for THIS patient') to a UTILITARIAN disaster ethic: do the GREATEST GOOD for the GREATEST NUMBER with limited resources. This reframing is the hardest part of disaster nursing and drives every triage decision. TRIAGE must be RAPID (seconds per patient), repeatable, and dynamic. START (Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment) is the most widely used adult MCI triage system; remember 'RPM' = Respirations, Perfusion, Mental status (the '30-2-Can Do' check): STEP 1 — ABILITY TO WALK: anyone who can WALK is tagged MINIMAL/MINOR (GREEN, 'walking wounded') and directed to a collection area (this quickly clears the lower-acuity group). STEP 2 — RESPIRATIONS (for non-ambulatory): if NOT breathing, OPEN/REPOSITION the airway once; if breathing resumes → IMMEDIATE (RED); if STILL not breathing → EXPECTANT/DECEASED (BLACK). If breathing with a rate >30/min → IMMEDIATE (RED). If breathing ≤30/min → go to perfusion. STEP 3 — PERFUSION: if the RADIAL PULSE is ABSENT (or capillary refill >2 seconds) → IMMEDIATE (RED) and control major hemorrhage; if perfusion is adequate → go to mental status. STEP 4 — MENTAL STATUS: if the patient CANNOT follow simple commands → IMMEDIATE (RED); if they CAN follow commands → DELAYED (YELLOW). TRIAGE CATEGORIES/colors: RED (IMMEDIATE) = life-threatening but SURVIVABLE injuries needing immediate intervention (airway compromise, major hemorrhage, tension pneumothorax, shock) — treated/transported FIRST; YELLOW (DELAYED) = serious injuries that can tolerate a delay (stable fractures, non-life-threatening wounds); GREEN (MINIMAL/MINOR) = 'walking wounded,' minor injuries; BLACK (EXPECTANT/DECEASED) = dead, OR injuries so severe that survival is unlikely given current resources (e.g., remains apneic after airway repositioning, devastating injuries) — these receive COMFORT care, NOT resuscitation. THE EXPECTANT CATEGORY IS THE KEY ETHICAL REVERSAL: a patient who in a normal single-patient setting would receive full resuscitation (e.g., a traumatic/apneic arrest) is, in an MCI, triaged EXPECTANT so that finite resources are directed to SALVAGEABLE patients — this is appropriate disaster practice, not abandonment, and is emotionally difficult for nurses. MINIMAL LIFE-SAVING INTERVENTIONS ONLY during primary triage: open the airway (reposition ± nasopharyngeal airway), control MASSIVE external hemorrhage (direct pressure/TOURNIQUET), needle decompression of a tension pneumothorax, and (in a chemical event) give antidote auto-injectors — each takes seconds; DO NOT stop to perform CPR or prolonged interventions during primary triage, because doing so diverts you from many salvageable patients (CPR in an MCI primary-triage context is generally not performed). RE-TRIAGE is essential: triage is DYNAMIC — patients are reassessed as their condition changes and as resources/personnel arrive, and categories are revised. PEDIATRIC triage uses JumpSTART for children (~1-8 yr / pre-pubertal), which is modified to reflect that children more often arrest from a PRIMARY RESPIRATORY cause: if a non-ambulatory child is APNEIC, open the airway; if still apneic, CHECK A PERIPHERAL PULSE — if a pulse is present, give 5 RESCUE BREATHS (an extra step not in adult START); if breathing resumes → IMMEDIATE; if still apneic after rescue breaths → EXPECTANT; respiratory-rate cutoffs (<15 or >45 = Immediate) and AVPU mental-status assessment are used (P-posturing or U → Immediate); infants who can't walk are assessed on the litter. INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM (ICS) / NIMS: the response operates under an organized command structure with defined roles (incident commander, triage/treatment/transport officers, etc.); the nurse WORKS WITHIN ICS, follows the chain of command, communicates clearly, documents/tracks patients (triage tags), and does NOT 'freelance.' SCENE SAFETY FIRST: the responder must NOT become a casualty — ensure the scene is secure (traffic, fire, structural collapse, ongoing violence, hazardous materials), wear appropriate PPE, and stage until safe; in HAZMAT/chemical events, DECONTAMINATION precedes definitive care. HOSPITAL/SYSTEM SURGE: activate the emergency operations/disaster plan, expand SURGE capacity, perform DECONTAMINATION when indicated, consider REVERSE TRIAGE (rapidly discharging/transferring stable inpatients to free beds), recall staff, conserve/allocate scarce resources (ventilators, blood, OR time) under crisis standards of care, and coordinate patient distribution/transport. LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION: an experienced RN often leads triage or a treatment area — assign roles by SCOPE and COMPETENCY (unlicensed assistive personnel can move/transport patients, act as litter-bearers, take vital signs, and provide comfort/basic first aid; LPN/LVN within their scope; the RN performs triage categorization, complex assessment, and critical interventions and supervises), use clear, closed-loop communication, track and document, and lead within ICS. PSYCHOLOGICAL impact is significant for both victims (acute stress) and RESPONDERS — provide psychological first aid, plan family REUNIFICATION, and arrange critical-incident stress debriefing/support afterward. NURSING ROLE: ensure scene safety, adopt the greatest-good mindset, triage rapidly and accurately with START/JumpSTART, perform only minimal life-saving maneuvers, re-triage, lead and delegate within incident command, and support the psychological needs of victims and staff

patient.name: MCI — Highway Bus Crash (~25 casualties)
patient.code: Mass-casualty incident: disaster (utilitarian) framework — greatest good for the greatest number; operate under Incident Command; individual full-resuscitation expectations are modified by resource limits (expectant category)
patient.admitDate: An experienced ED RN is among the first qualified responders to a multi-vehicle highway crash involving a passenger BUS, with approximately 25 casualties of mixed ages (adults and several children) scattered across the roadway. EMS and fire are establishing Incident Command; the scene is being secured (traffic stopped, no active fire/hazmat identified). The RN is directed to begin/assist field TRIAGE using START (and JumpSTART for children), categorize casualties, perform only rapid life-saving maneuvers, and help organize treatment/transport — shifting from individual-patient care to population-level triage.
patient.room: Field triage zone → casualty collection points (Immediate/Delayed/Minimal/Expectant) under Incident Command; receiving ED activating its mass-casualty/surge plan

vitals.temp: —; a young adult with a forearm laceration who is WALKING and asking to help others → can ambulate = MINIMAL (GREEN, 'walking wounded'); directed to the green collection area for later care; recruited to apply pressure to his own wound

vitals.rr: apneic on first check → airway REPOSITIONED → spontaneous breathing resumes
vitals.temp: —; non-ambulatory adult found apneic; a single airway-opening maneuver (reposition ± NPA) restored breathing → tag IMMEDIATE (RED); a quick, seconds-long maneuver that saved a salvageable life without diverting prolonged effort

vitals.rr: apneic; remains apneic after airway repositioning
vitals.temp: —; non-ambulatory adult with devastating injuries who REMAINS APNEIC after the airway is opened → EXPECTANT/DECEASED (BLACK); receives comfort measures, NOT CPR/resuscitation, so resources go to salvageable casualties (the key MCI ethical reversal)

vitals.hr: weak/absent radial pulse; cap refill >3 sec
vitals.temp: —; non-ambulatory adult breathing >30/min with an absent radial pulse and a bleeding thigh wound → IMMEDIATE (RED); apply a TOURNIQUET to control massive hemorrhage (a rapid life-saving maneuver) and prioritize for immediate treatment/transport

—; non-ambulatory adult with a closed deformed lower leg (likely fracture), RR ≤30, radial pulse present, cap refill <2 sec, FOLLOWS commands → DELAYED (YELLOW); splint/comfort and transport after the Immediate group

vitals.rr: apneic on first check; after airway opened still apneic, but HAS a peripheral pulse → 5 RESCUE BREATHS given → breathing resumes
vitals.temp: —; a young child found non-ambulatory and apneic with a pulse → per JumpSTART, give 5 RESCUE BREATHS (the pediatric step absent from adult START); breathing resumed → IMMEDIATE (RED). Had the child remained apneic after rescue breaths → EXPECTANT

labs.n: The MCI mindset + scene safety + incident command
labs.v: A MASS-CASUALTY INCIDENT (MCI) overwhelms available resources, requiring a fundamental shift from the everyday goal ('do EVERYTHING for THIS patient') to the disaster ethic of the GREATEST GOOD FOR THE GREATEST NUMBER (utilitarian allocation of finite resources). This is the single most important conceptual change and underlies every triage decision. BEFORE touching any patient: (1) SCENE SAFETY — the responder must NOT become another casualty; ensure the scene is secure (stopped traffic, no active fire, structural stability, no ongoing violence/active threat, and no hazardous materials); wear appropriate PPE; if HAZMAT/chemical exposure is involved, DECONTAMINATION precedes definitive care and responders use proper protective equipment; stage in a safe area until cleared. (2) INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM (ICS)/NIMS — disasters are managed under an organized command structure with defined roles (Incident Commander; Triage, Treatment, and Transport Officers; etc.); the nurse INTEGRATES into ICS, follows the chain of command, communicates concisely, and does NOT 'freelance' (independent, uncoordinated action fragments the response and endangers everyone). The nurse identifies to command, accepts an assignment (e.g., triage officer or treatment-area lead), and reports needs/status up the chain. (3) The receiving HOSPITAL simultaneously activates its emergency operations/disaster plan, expands SURGE capacity, sets up DECONTAMINATION if needed, may use REVERSE TRIAGE (rapidly discharging/transferring stable inpatients to create capacity), recalls staff, and coordinates resource allocation + patient distribution. THIS SCENARIO: an experienced ED RN is among the first qualified responders to a highway bus crash with ~25 mixed-age casualties; EMS/fire are establishing ICS and securing the scene; the RN confirms scene safety, reports to Incident Command, accepts a triage assignment, and begins START/JumpSTART triage with the greatest-good mindset.
labs.r: MCI = resources overwhelmed → shift to greatest good for the greatest number. FIRST ensure SCENE SAFETY (don't become a victim; PPE; decon if hazmat) and INTEGRATE into Incident Command (accept a role, follow the chain, don't freelance). Hospital activates surge/disaster plan + reverse triage. RN role: safety + command integration + adopt the utilitarian triage mindset.

labs.v: START (Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment) categorizes adult casualties in SECONDS using 'RPM' (Respirations, Perfusion, Mental status), the '30-2-Can Do' check: STEP 1 — CAN THE PATIENT WALK? Anyone who can WALK is MINIMAL/MINOR (GREEN, 'walking wounded') → direct them to a collection area; this instantly separates the large lower-acuity group and frees you to focus on the non-ambulatory. STEP 2 — RESPIRATIONS (non-ambulatory): if NOT breathing, OPEN/REPOSITION the airway ONCE (± nasopharyngeal airway); if breathing RESUMES → IMMEDIATE (RED); if the patient remains APNEIC → EXPECTANT/DECEASED (BLACK). If breathing at a RATE >30/min → IMMEDIATE (RED). If breathing ≤30/min → assess perfusion. STEP 3 — PERFUSION: check the RADIAL PULSE (and/or capillary refill); if the radial pulse is ABSENT (or cap refill >2 sec) → IMMEDIATE (RED) and control major hemorrhage; if perfusion is adequate (radial pulse present, cap refill <2 sec) → assess mental status. STEP 4 — MENTAL STATUS: ask the patient to follow a SIMPLE COMMAND; if they CANNOT obey commands → IMMEDIATE (RED); if they CAN follow commands → DELAYED (YELLOW). The mnemonic '30-2-Can Do' = RR <30, cap refill <2 sec, and can-do (follows commands) → DELAYED; failing ANY criterion → IMMEDIATE. Triage decisions are made in ~30-60 seconds per patient; you TAG and MOVE ON (you do not stop to provide ongoing care during primary triage). THIS SCENARIO illustrates each branch: an ambulatory laceration = GREEN; an apneic patient who breathes after airway opening = RED; one who remains apneic = BLACK; a patient breathing >30 with no radial pulse + a bleeding wound = RED (tourniquet); a stable splintable fracture who follows commands = YELLOW.
labs.r: START ('RPM'/30-2-Can Do): (1) Walk? → GREEN. (2) Breathing? not breathing → open airway → if breathes RED, if still apneic BLACK; RR >30 → RED. (3) Perfusion? absent radial pulse / cap refill >2s → RED. (4) Mental status? can't follow commands → RED; follows commands → YELLOW. ~30-60 sec/patient; tag and move on. RN role: rapid, accurate categorization.

labs.v: TRIAGE CATEGORIES (colors) and how they drive treatment/transport order: RED — IMMEDIATE: life-threatening but SURVIVABLE injuries requiring immediate intervention (airway obstruction, major/uncontrolled hemorrhage, tension pneumothorax, decompensated shock); treated and transported FIRST. YELLOW — DELAYED: serious injuries that can tolerate a delay without significant deterioration (stable fractures, large but controlled wounds, abdominal injuries with stable vitals); treated/transported after the Immediate group. GREEN — MINIMAL/MINOR: the 'walking wounded' with minor injuries (minor lacerations, sprains); can wait the longest and can sometimes assist. BLACK — EXPECTANT/DECEASED: those who are dead OR whose injuries are so severe that survival is unlikely given the available resources (remains apneic after airway opening, devastating non-survivable injuries) → provide COMFORT care (positioning, analgesia, presence) but NOT resuscitation. THE EXPECTANT CATEGORY is the defining ethical reversal of disaster care: in normal single-patient practice, an apneic/pulseless trauma patient would receive full resuscitation; in an MCI, expending a team and scarce resources on one likely-unsalvageable patient would cost the lives of several salvageable RED patients — so they are triaged EXPECTANT. This is correct, evidence-based disaster practice (not abandonment), and is emotionally difficult — nurses need support around it. MINIMAL LIFE-SAVING INTERVENTIONS are the ONLY treatments performed DURING primary triage, each taking seconds: (1) OPEN THE AIRWAY (reposition ± nasopharyngeal airway); (2) CONTROL MASSIVE EXTERNAL HEMORRHAGE (direct pressure/TOURNIQUET); (3) NEEDLE DECOMPRESSION of a tension pneumothorax; (4) in a chemical event, administer ANTIDOTE auto-injectors (e.g., for nerve agents). You do NOT perform CPR or prolonged resuscitation during primary triage (it immobilizes rescuers and resources away from many salvageable victims). RE-TRIAGE: triage is DYNAMIC — casualties are REASSESSED as their condition evolves and as more personnel/resources arrive; a Delayed patient who deteriorates becomes Immediate, and categories are continually updated. Accurate TAGGING/DOCUMENTATION and tracking support handoffs and transport decisions.
labs.r: Colors: RED Immediate (survivable life threats — first), YELLOW Delayed (serious but can wait), GREEN Minimal (walking wounded), BLACK Expectant/Deceased (dead or unsalvageable given resources → comfort care, not resuscitation — the key ethical reversal). During triage do ONLY minimal maneuvers: open airway, stop massive bleeding (tourniquet), needle-decompress, antidote auto-injectors — NO CPR. RE-TRIAGE dynamically. RN role: categorize + minimal interventions + reassess.

labs.v: JumpSTART is the pediatric MCI triage tool (for children roughly 1-8 years / those who appear pre-pubertal; adults and adolescents who look like adults use START). It is MODIFIED from START because children more often deteriorate/arrest from a PRIMARY RESPIRATORY cause (and may be salvageable with a few rescue breaths), so it adds a rescue-breath step that adult START does not have. ALGORITHM: STEP 1 — if the child can WALK → MINIMAL (GREEN); infants/children who cannot walk (including those carried) are assessed on the litter. STEP 2 — RESPIRATIONS: if breathing, assess rate — RR <15 or >45/min → IMMEDIATE (RED); RR 15-45 → assess perfusion. If APNEIC (or very irregular), OPEN the airway; if breathing resumes → IMMEDIATE. If STILL APNEIC after opening the airway → CHECK A PERIPHERAL PULSE: if there is NO pulse → EXPECTANT/DECEASED (BLACK); if a pulse IS present → give 5 RESCUE BREATHS (the distinctive pediatric step) — if breathing resumes → IMMEDIATE; if the child remains apneic after the 5 rescue breaths → EXPECTANT (BLACK). STEP 3 — PERFUSION: check a peripheral pulse; if ABSENT → IMMEDIATE (RED); if present → assess mental status. STEP 4 — MENTAL STATUS (AVPU): Alert/responds to Voice/responds appropriately to Pain → DELAYED (YELLOW); INAPPROPRIATE response to pain (posturing) or Unresponsive → IMMEDIATE (RED). KEY POINT: the rescue-breath step reflects pediatric physiology (respiratory arrest precedes circulatory arrest) and gives apneic-but-perfusing children a brief chance — distinguishing JumpSTART from the adult algorithm where an apneic patient who doesn't resume breathing after airway opening is Expectant. OTHER SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS: family REUNIFICATION + tracking of children (who may be nonverbal/unidentified); developmentally appropriate comfort; and the heightened emotional toll on responders when triaging children, including assigning a child to the Expectant category.
labs.r: JumpSTART (children ~1-8 yr): like START but adds a RESCUE-BREATH step because kids arrest from primary respiratory causes — if apneic after opening the airway BUT has a pulse, give 5 rescue breaths (resumes → Immediate; still apneic → Expectant; no pulse → Expectant). RR <15 or >45 → Immediate; AVPU for mental status. Plus child tracking/reunification + responder emotional toll. RN role: use the correct age-appropriate tool.

labs.v: An experienced RN frequently LEADS triage or a treatment area in an MCI, making LEADERSHIP and DELEGATION central. Within the INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM, the nurse accepts a defined ROLE (e.g., Triage Officer, Treatment-Area Lead, or Transport coordination) and works within the chain of command with clear, CLOSED-LOOP communication. DELEGATION must match SCOPE and COMPETENCY: UNLICENSED ASSISTIVE PERSONNEL (UAP)/first-aid responders/litter-bearers can MOVE and TRANSPORT patients, carry litters, take vital signs, apply direct pressure, and provide comfort/basic first aid; LPN/LVNs perform within their scope (e.g., established interventions, wound care, monitoring) under RN direction; the REGISTERED NURSE performs the TRIAGE CATEGORIZATION, complex assessment, and critical interventions, and SUPERVISES the team — triage decision-making itself is an RN-level judgment and is not delegated to unlicensed staff. The leader assigns tasks explicitly ('You—apply this tourniquet and stay with this red patient'; 'You two—move green-tagged patients to the collection point'), tracks and documents casualties (TRIAGE TAGS, counts, dispositions) for accurate handoff and transport, allocates scarce resources, and continually reassesses/re-triages and reallocates as personnel and supplies arrive. The leader also COMMUNICATES needs and status UP to Incident Command (numbers by category, resource/transport needs) and disseminates instructions DOWN to the team. CRITICALLY, the nurse does NOT 'freelance' (independent uncoordinated heroics—e.g., stopping to do prolonged CPR on one expectant patient—fragment the response and cost salvageable lives). Effective MCI leadership = the right people doing the right tasks within their scope, coordinated under command, with continuous reassessment. PSYCHOLOGICAL leadership matters too: brief and support the team, normalize the difficulty of disaster decisions (especially expectant triage and pediatric casualties), plan family reunification, and arrange post-incident debriefing/critical-incident stress support.
labs.r: MCI leadership/delegation: accept an ICS role + work the chain of command with closed-loop communication; delegate by scope (UAP = move/transport/vitals/comfort/pressure; LPN within scope; RN = triage categorization + complex care + supervision — triage judgment is NOT delegated to UAP); assign tasks explicitly, track/document (tags), allocate resources, re-triage, and report up/instruct down; do NOT freelance. Support the team psychologically. RN role: lead + delegate within command.

labs.n: Disaster ethics, system surge, and Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: DISASTER ETHICS: MCI care is UTILITARIAN — maximize lives saved with finite resources (greatest good for the greatest number), which legitimately MODIFIES normal individual-centered care: the EXPECTANT category withholds resuscitation from the likely-unsalvageable so RED (salvageable) patients live; scarce resources (ventilators, blood, OR time, ICU beds) are allocated under CRISIS STANDARDS OF CARE using objective criteria, not first-come-first-served; and triage is repeated as the situation evolves. This is ethically appropriate and protected by disaster frameworks—but it is emotionally and morally hard, so responders need support and clear protocols. SYSTEM/HOSPITAL SURGE: activate the emergency operations plan + Hospital Incident Command; expand SURGE capacity (space, staff, supplies); DECONTAMINATE before entry in chemical/hazmat events; use REVERSE TRIAGE (rapidly discharge/transfer stable inpatients to open beds); recall off-duty staff; conserve/allocate scarce resources; and coordinate patient distribution across facilities to avoid overwhelming one hospital. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct disaster practice): the nurse did NOT rush in before ensuring SCENE SAFETY/PPE (avoiding becoming a casualty); did NOT 'freelance' outside Incident Command; did NOT stop to perform prolonged CPR/resuscitation on an expectant patient during primary triage (which would have cost salvageable lives); did NOT apply everyday single-patient standards (full resuscitation for everyone) that the resources cannot support; did NOT delegate TRIAGE CATEGORIZATION to unlicensed staff; did NOT use adult START on the young child (used JumpSTART with its rescue-breath step); and did NOT neglect re-triage. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: an experienced RN adopting the greatest-good mindset; early, organized Incident Command + scene security; rapid, accurate START/JumpSTART triage with appropriate minimal life-saving maneuvers (airway, tourniquet, rescue breaths for a perfusing apneic child); clear delegation within scope; dynamic re-triage; hospital surge/reverse-triage activation; accurate tagging/tracking + family reunification planning; and post-incident psychological support/debriefing for responders. EXPECTED outcome with correct execution: the maximum number of salvageable casualties are identified, stabilized with minimal maneuvers, and prioritized for treatment/transport, while expectant patients receive humane comfort care—achieving the disaster goal of saving the most lives possible, and supporting the well-being of responders afterward.
labs.r: Disaster ethics = utilitarian (most lives saved); expectant category + crisis standards + scarce-resource allocation are appropriate, not abandonment. System surge: activate disaster plan/Hospital ICS, decon, reverse triage, recall staff, distribute patients. Negatives confirm correct practice (scene safety first, no freelancing, no CPR on expectant during triage, JumpSTART for the child, triage not delegated to UAP, re-triage). Protective: greatest-good mindset + ICS + rapid accurate triage + minimal maneuvers + delegation + surge + debriefing. RN role: ethical, organized, life-maximizing response + responder support.

nursesNote: Field response — an experienced ED RN is among the first qualified responders to a multi-vehicle HIGHWAY BUS CRASH with ~25 casualties of MIXED AGES (adults + several children). FIRST: confirmed SCENE SAFETY (traffic stopped, no active fire/hazmat, structurally stable, no ongoing threat) + donned PPE — does NOT become a casualty; reported to and INTEGRATED into INCIDENT COMMAND (EMS/fire establishing ICS), accepted a TRIAGE OFFICER assignment, and adopted the MCI mindset — GREATEST GOOD FOR THE GREATEST NUMBER (utilitarian), not everyday individual full-care. Began START (adults) / JumpSTART (children) triage, ~30-60 sec each, tagging and moving on, doing ONLY minimal life-saving maneuvers. CASUALTY A: young adult with forearm laceration, WALKING → MINIMAL (GREEN); directed to green collection area + asked to hold pressure on his own wound. CASUALTY B: non-ambulatory adult APNEIC on first check → single airway REPOSITION (± NPA) → breathing resumed → IMMEDIATE (RED) (seconds-long maneuver, salvageable life). CASUALTY C: non-ambulatory adult with devastating injuries REMAINS APNEIC after airway opened → EXPECTANT/DECEASED (BLACK) → comfort care, NOT CPR/resuscitation (the key MCI ethical reversal — resources to salvageable patients). CASUALTY D: non-ambulatory adult breathing >30/min + ABSENT radial pulse + bleeding thigh → IMMEDIATE (RED) + TOURNIQUET applied to control massive hemorrhage (rapid life-saving maneuver). CASUALTY E: non-ambulatory adult with closed deformed lower leg, RR ≤30, radial pulse present, cap refill <2s, FOLLOWS commands → DELAYED (YELLOW) → splint/comfort, transport after Immediates. CASUALTY F (CHILD): non-ambulatory + apneic with a peripheral PULSE → per JumpSTART gave 5 RESCUE BREATHS (pediatric step not in adult START) → breathing resumed → IMMEDIATE (RED) (had he remained apneic after rescue breaths → EXPECTANT). DELEGATION within scope: UAP/litter-bearers assigned to MOVE/transport patients + take vitals + apply direct pressure + comfort; LPN within scope under RN direction; RN retained TRIAGE CATEGORIZATION + complex assessment + critical interventions + supervision (triage judgment NOT delegated to unlicensed staff); explicit closed-loop task assignments; casualties TAGGED + tracked/documented; needs/counts reported UP to Incident Command; did NOT freelance (no prolonged CPR on an expectant patient). RE-TRIAGE performed dynamically as condition/resources changed. Receiving hospital activated its mass-casualty/SURGE plan (Hospital ICS, surge capacity, decon readiness, REVERSE TRIAGE to open beds, staff recall, resource allocation, patient distribution). Disaster ethics applied (expectant category + crisis standards). Afterward: family reunification + psychological first aid for victims + critical-incident stress debriefing/support for responders planned. Outcome: maximum salvageable casualties identified + stabilized with minimal maneuvers + prioritized for treatment/transport; expectant patients given humane comfort care.
providerOrders: MASS-CASUALTY INCIDENT RESPONSE — operate under Incident Command System (ICS)/NIMS
INCIDENT: highway bus crash, ~25 casualties of mixed ages (adults + children); field triage + treatment + transport organization
MINDSET: disaster (utilitarian) framework — greatest good for the greatest number with finite resources; crisis standards of care
SCENE SAFETY (before patient contact):
Ensure the scene is secure (traffic, fire, structural stability, no active threat/violence, no hazardous materials); do not become a casualty
Don appropriate PPE; in hazmat/chemical events, decontaminate before definitive care; stage until safe
INTEGRATE INTO INCIDENT COMMAND:
Report to Incident Command; accept a defined role (e.g., Triage Officer / Treatment-Area Lead); follow the chain of command; do NOT freelance
Communicate clearly (closed-loop); report casualty counts by category + resource/transport needs up the chain
TRIAGE — START (adults) / JumpSTART (children); ~30-60 sec each, tag and move on:

START: (1) can walk → MINIMAL/GREEN; (2) breathing — not breathing → open airway → breathes = IMMEDIATE/RED, still apneic = EXPECTANT/BLACK; RR >30 = IMMEDIATE/RED; (3) perfusion — absent radial pulse or cap refill >2s = IMMEDIATE/RED (control hemorrhage); (4) mental status — can't follow commands = IMMEDIATE/RED, follows commands = DELAYED/YELLOW

JumpSTART (children ~1-8 yr): if apneic after opening airway but has a pulse → give 5 RESCUE BREATHS (resumes = IMMEDIATE; still apneic = EXPECTANT; no pulse = EXPECTANT); RR <15 or >45 = IMMEDIATE; AVPU for mental status
Categories drive order: RED (immediate, treat/transport first) → YELLOW (delayed) → GREEN (minimal) → BLACK (expectant/deceased — comfort care, not resuscitation)
MINIMAL LIFE-SAVING INTERVENTIONS ONLY during primary triage:
Open airway (reposition ± nasopharyngeal airway); control massive hemorrhage (direct pressure/tourniquet); needle-decompress tension pneumothorax; antidote auto-injectors (chemical events)
Do NOT perform CPR or prolonged resuscitation during primary triage
RE-TRIAGE + TRACKING:
Reassess casualties dynamically as condition/resources change; update categories
Apply triage tags; document/track patients + dispositions for handoff and transport
DELEGATION (by scope + competency):
UAP/litter-bearers: move/transport patients, take vital signs, apply direct pressure, provide comfort/basic first aid
LPN/LVN: within scope under RN direction; RN: triage categorization, complex assessment, critical interventions, supervision (do NOT delegate triage judgment to unlicensed staff)
HOSPITAL/SYSTEM SURGE:
Activate emergency operations/disaster plan + Hospital Incident Command; expand surge capacity; set up decontamination if indicated
Reverse triage (discharge/transfer stable inpatients to open beds); recall staff; allocate scarce resources; coordinate patient distribution/transport
PSYCHOLOGICAL + REUNIFICATION:
Psychological first aid for victims; family reunification + tracking (especially children); support staff
Post-incident critical-incident stress debriefing/support for responders

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters when arriving at a mass-casualty incident?
steps.inst: Select ALL considerations that are critical for the responding nurse.

opts.text: Scene safety FIRST — ensure the scene is secure (traffic/fire/structural/violence/hazmat) and don PPE before patient contact; the responder must not become a casualty
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — responder safety precedes care; a downed rescuer adds a casualty and removes a helper. RN role: scene safety + PPE first.

opts.text: Adopt the MCI mindset — shift from 'do everything for this individual' to the greatest good for the greatest number with finite resources
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the utilitarian mindset drives every triage decision. RN role: reframe to population-level triage.

opts.text: Integrate into the Incident Command System — report in, accept a defined role, follow the chain of command, and do not freelance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — coordinated command prevents a fragmented, dangerous response. RN role: work within ICS.

opts.text: Use rapid START (adults) / JumpSTART (children) triage — ~30-60 seconds per patient, categorize, tag, and move on
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rapid, systematic triage is how limited responders sort many casualties. RN role: fast, accurate categorization.

opts.text: Perform only minimal life-saving maneuvers during triage (open airway, control massive hemorrhage, needle-decompress, antidote auto-injectors) — not prolonged care or CPR
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — quick maneuvers save salvageable lives without diverting from many others. RN role: minimal interventions during triage.

opts.text: Recognize the EXPECTANT (black) category — patients unsalvageable given resources receive comfort care, not resuscitation, so resources go to salvageable patients
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the expectant category is the key ethical reversal of disaster care. RN role: appropriate, humane expectant triage.

opts.text: Delegate by scope/competency and the receiving hospital activates surge (reverse triage, recall staff, decon) — organize people and system resources
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — leadership/delegation + system surge multiply effective capacity. RN role: delegate appropriately + support surge.

opts.text: Begin full individual assessments and definitive treatment on the first critically injured patient you reach before triaging the others
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Stopping for full care/definitive treatment of one patient during primary triage leaves many salvageable casualties unassessed and untreated. Triage ALL first (seconds each), then treat by priority.

opts.text: Start CPR on a casualty who is apneic and pulseless after the airway is opened, continuing resuscitation at the scene
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG in an MCI. A casualty who remains apneic after airway repositioning is EXPECTANT (black); performing prolonged CPR ties up rescuers/resources and costs salvageable RED patients their lives. Provide comfort care and move on.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate statements about MCI triage.

opts.text: In START, anyone who can WALK is tagged MINIMAL (green); this quickly separates the 'walking wounded' so responders focus on the non-ambulatory
opts.rat: CORRECT — the ambulatory-first step. Foundation for rapid sorting.

opts.text: A non-ambulatory casualty who is apneic but resumes breathing after the airway is repositioned is IMMEDIATE (red); one who remains apneic is EXPECTANT (black)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the respirations branch + the airway-opening maneuver. Foundation for the red/black distinction.

opts.text: Under START, RR >30/min, an absent radial pulse (or cap refill >2 sec), or inability to follow commands each makes a breathing casualty IMMEDIATE (red); RR <30 + cap refill <2 sec + follows commands = DELAYED (yellow)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the RPM/30-2-Can Do criteria. Foundation for red vs yellow.

opts.text: JumpSTART adds a rescue-breath step: an apneic child WITH a pulse gets 5 rescue breaths (because children arrest from primary respiratory causes) before being classified
opts.rat: CORRECT — the key pediatric modification. Foundation for correct child triage.

opts.text: Triage is dynamic — casualties are re-triaged as their condition changes and resources arrive, and categories are updated
opts.rat: CORRECT — re-triage principle. Foundation for ongoing accuracy.

opts.text: Triage categorization is an RN-level judgment; tasks like moving patients, taking vitals, and applying pressure can be delegated to UAP, but the triage decision is not
opts.rat: CORRECT — scope/delegation principle. Foundation for appropriate task assignment.

opts.text: In an MCI you should treat casualties strictly in the order you encounter them (first-come, first-served), regardless of injury severity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. MCI triage is by acuity/salvageability (Immediate first), not first-come-first-served; the whole point is to prioritize by who benefits most from limited resources.

opts.text: The same adult START algorithm (no rescue-breath step) should be applied to young children to keep triage consistent
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Children use JumpSTART, which adds the rescue-breath step for an apneic child with a pulse; applying adult START would wrongly classify a salvageable child as expectant.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these casualties in the order they should generally receive treatment/transport. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE (RED): breathing >30/min with an absent radial pulse and major hemorrhage controlled by tourniquet — a survivable life threat needing immediate intervention/transport
opts.rat: #1 — Immediate/red casualties (survivable life threats) are treated and transported first.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE (RED): apneic who resumed breathing after airway repositioning — salvageable, airway-dependent, needs immediate attention
opts.rat: #2 — Also Immediate/red (airway-dependent, salvageable); within the red group, airway/breathing instability is prioritized.

opts.text: DELAYED (YELLOW): closed deformed lower-leg fracture, perfusing, following commands — serious but can tolerate a delay
opts.rat: #3 — Delayed/yellow: treated/transported after the Immediate group.

opts.text: MINIMAL (GREEN): ambulatory forearm laceration ('walking wounded') — minor, can wait the longest and may assist
opts.rat: #4 — Minimal/green: lowest priority for treatment/transport; can wait.

opts.text: EXPECTANT (BLACK): remains apneic after airway opening with devastating injuries — comfort care only; not allocated scarce resuscitation resources
opts.rat: #5 — Expectant/black: not prioritized for scarce resuscitation resources in an MCI; receives comfort care so salvageable patients are saved.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED during this MCI response.

opts.text: Confirm scene safety + don PPE, report to Incident Command, accept a triage role, and begin START/JumpSTART triage
opts.rat: Correct opening sequence — safety + command integration + systematic triage. RN role: safety + ICS + triage.

opts.text: Direct ambulatory casualties to a green collection area to quickly separate the walking wounded
opts.rat: Standard START step-1 sorting. RN role: rapidly clear the ambulatory group.

opts.text: Open the airway of an apneic non-ambulatory casualty once; tag IMMEDIATE if breathing resumes, EXPECTANT if it does not
opts.rat: Correct respirations branch with the single airway maneuver. RN role: minimal maneuver + categorize.

opts.text: Apply a tourniquet to control major extremity hemorrhage during triage of a red casualty
opts.rat: A rapid, life-saving minimal intervention permitted during triage. RN role: stop massive bleeding.

opts.text: For an apneic child with a pulse, give 5 rescue breaths (JumpSTART) before assigning a category
opts.rat: Correct pediatric step reflecting respiratory-driven pediatric arrest. RN role: use JumpSTART for children.

opts.text: Delegate moving/transporting patients, taking vitals, and applying pressure to UAP/litter-bearers while the RN performs triage categorization and supervises
opts.rat: Appropriate scope-based delegation. RN role: delegate tasks; retain triage judgment + supervision.

opts.text: Re-triage casualties as conditions change and resources arrive, updating categories and tags
opts.rat: Triage is dynamic. RN role: continuous reassessment.

opts.text: Stop at the first critically injured patient and provide full definitive resuscitation before assessing anyone else
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — full definitive care on one patient during primary triage abandons many salvageable casualties. Triage all rapidly first, then treat by priority.

opts.text: Perform prolonged CPR at the scene on a casualty who is apneic and pulseless after airway repositioning
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG in an MCI — that casualty is expectant; CPR ties up rescuers/resources and costs salvageable lives. Provide comfort care and move on.

opts.text: Assign triage categorization decisions to unlicensed assistive personnel to speed things up
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — triage categorization is an RN-level clinical judgment and cannot be delegated to UAP (who may move patients, take vitals, apply pressure).

opts.text: Enter an unsecured/hazardous scene immediately without PPE to reach victims faster
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — entering an unsafe scene risks making the responder a casualty (and removing a rescuer). Scene safety + PPE come first.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the responding nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure scene safety and don appropriate PPE (do not become a casualty)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Responder safety precedes all care. RN role: scene safety + PPE.

opts.text: Report to and integrate with Incident Command; accept a triage/treatment role within the chain of command
opts.rat: #2 — Second. A coordinated, commanded response prevents chaos. RN role: ICS integration.

opts.text: Perform rapid START/JumpSTART triage of all casualties, tagging categories and giving only minimal life-saving maneuvers (airway, hemorrhage control, rescue breaths for a perfusing apneic child)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Systematic triage sorts the many casualties for prioritized care. RN role: rapid triage + minimal interventions.

opts.text: Delegate tasks by scope (UAP move/transport/vitals/pressure), organize treatment by category (red first), and track/tag patients for transport
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Leadership/delegation + organized treatment/transport operationalize the triage. RN role: delegate + organize + track.

opts.text: Re-triage dynamically, coordinate with the surging hospital, and arrange family reunification + responder debriefing/support afterward
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/after. Reassessment + system coordination + psychological support sustain the response. RN role: re-triage + coordination + support.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The nurse confirmed scene safety, donned PPE, reported to Incident Command, accepted a triage role, and began START/JumpSTART with a greatest-good mindset
opts.rat: Correct opening sequence (safety + command + systematic triage + utilitarian mindset). Model handling.

opts.text: An ambulatory casualty was tagged green and directed to a collection area; a perfusing apneic child received 5 rescue breaths per JumpSTART and was tagged red when breathing resumed
opts.rat: Correct ambulatory sorting + correct pediatric (JumpSTART) rescue-breath step. Model handling.

opts.text: A casualty breathing >30/min with no radial pulse and a bleeding wound was tagged immediate and a tourniquet was applied during triage
opts.rat: Correct red categorization + an appropriate minimal life-saving maneuver (hemorrhage control). Model handling.

opts.text: UAP/litter-bearers were assigned to move patients, take vitals, and apply pressure, while the RN retained triage categorization and supervision
opts.rat: Appropriate scope-based delegation with the RN retaining triage judgment. Model handling.

opts.text: The nurse stopped to perform prolonged CPR on a casualty who remained apneic and pulseless after airway opening, while other red casualties waited
opts.rat: ⚠️ That casualty is expectant in an MCI; prolonged CPR diverted the nurse from salvageable red patients. Provide comfort care and continue triage.

opts.text: The nurse entered the unsecured scene without PPE to reach victims faster and was injured by passing traffic
opts.rat: ⚠️ Scene safety/PPE come first — the responder became a casualty, adding harm and subtracting a rescuer.

opts.text: Triage categorization decisions were handed off to an unlicensed volunteer to move faster
opts.rat: ⚠️ Triage categorization is an RN-level judgment and cannot be delegated to unlicensed staff; misclassification costs lives.

opts.text: Casualties were re-triaged as conditions changed, tagged/tracked, and the receiving hospital activated surge + reverse triage to create capacity
opts.rat: Dynamic re-triage + tracking + system surge are correct disaster practice. Model handling.

opts.text: The nurse treated casualties strictly first-come-first-served regardless of severity
opts.rat: ⚠️ MCI care prioritizes by acuity/salvageability (immediate first), not first-come-first-served.

opts.text: After the incident, family reunification was coordinated and a critical-incident stress debriefing was arranged for responders
opts.rat: Reunification + responder psychological support are appropriate, important parts of the response. Model handling.
